crux_reading: HIGHER — AWS sustains 25%+ growth through FY2027 on contracted backlog conversion; Trainium custom silicon structurally expands margins concurrent with growth; advertising compounds via Prime Video inventory layer

key_debates.debate: Does the $364bn AWS contracted backlog signal that 25%+ growth is durable through FY2027, or is the backlog surge a capacity-catch-up that frontloads commitments without guaranteeing conversion pace?
key_debates.bull_view: The $364bn backlog is a company-filed hard number, not an analyst extrapolation. It grew from $244bn to $364bn in a single quarter — a $120bn addition in 90 days — and carries a backlog-to-annualized-revenue ratio of ~2.4x against a $150bn annualized run rate. Historical cloud backlog-to-revenue conversion at AWS runs at 80-85% over 36 months (per Amazon's own disclosure methodology). Applied literally: the existing backlog alone supports $291-309bn of incremental revenue through FY2028 before a single new booking is counted. The Q1 2026 growth rate of 28% YoY was the fastest in 15 quarters, achieved while management stated that demand continues to exceed available capacity — meaning the constraint is supply (data center build-out), not demand. Amazon's $200bn FY2026 capex commitment is the capacity unlock. If that infrastructure comes online on the stated schedule, the math says AWS exits FY2026 at $166bn revenue (29% growth) and sustains 26%+ through FY2027. The backlog acceleration was not driven by one customer: CEO commentary confirmed breadth across multiple enterprise and AI-native customers, and the Anthropic commitment ($100bn+ over 10 years) contributes only ~$10bn per year — a meaningful but non-dominant share of the total pipeline.
key_debates.bear_view: Backlog is a contractual commitment with optionality. Multi-year enterprise cloud contracts include renegotiation rights, volume minimums rather than hard volume commitments, and cancellation provisions if AI ROI does not materialize at scale. Azure grew at 40% YoY in Q1 2026 versus AWS at 28%, implying Microsoft's OpenAI exclusivity advantage is pulling the fastest-growing AI workloads. A backlog surge in a single quarter could reflect customers locking in capacity commitments ahead of anticipated price increases rather than organic demand expansion.
key_debates.our_verdict: The backlog is the strongest single piece of forward evidence in the cloud sector. A $120bn quarterly addition is not a rounding error. The conversion risk is real but it requires a specific, observable mechanism — AI ROI collapse at enterprise scale — to invalidate. The base case already assumes deceleration to 17% by FY2028; the bull simply argues that deceleration is 5pp slower (22% vs 17%) because the contracted supply of workloads is larger than the base assumes. That is not a heroic assumption; it is the more literal read of the filed number.
key_debates.what_would_change_our_mind: AWS Q2 or Q3 2026 revenue growth decelerates sharply below 24% despite the backlog — which would signal either (a) capacity delays in the data center build-out are suppressing conversion, or (b) enterprise customers are consuming at a lower pace than the commitment cadence implies, potentially foreshadowing cancellations. The second signal is management commentary on backlog cancellation rates or renegotiation activity, which would be a first.
key_debates.worth_per_share: $15.96/share. Base AWS segment EV: $812.5bn ($75.53/share). Bull AWS segment EV: $984.2bn ($91.49/share). The delta is driven by AWS FY2028 NOPAT rising from $65.4bn (base: $230bn revenue × 36% × 79%) to $72.7bn (bull: $255.7bn revenue × 36% × 79%), applying the same Gordon exit multiple of 16.53x (g_lt 5.0%, ROIC 55%, r 10.5%), discounted 2 years at 10.5%. The $1bn of additional AWS EV translates to $0.093/share at 10.757bn shares.
key_debates.evidence_quality: CONTRACTED — the $364bn backlog is company-disclosed and filed. The conversion rate assumption (80-85% over 36 months) is extrapolated from Amazon's own backlog disclosure methodology and comparable Azure RPO conversion history.

key_debates.debate: Does Trainium custom silicon represent a structural margin expansion lever — reducing NVIDIA dependency and lifting AWS EBIT margin toward 39-40% as AI workload mix shifts — or is it a high-capital, high-execution bet that arrives too late to matter for the FY2028 normalization window?
key_debates.bull_view: Trainium is already a $20bn+ annualized revenue run rate growing triple-digit YoY, with $225bn+ in committed customer purchase obligations (company-disclosed). Those are not pilot contracts — they are signed, multi-year commitments from customers who have already adopted the silicon. The moat argument is structural: Trainium's purpose-built architecture for transformer training workloads reduces per-token training cost relative to NVIDIA H100/B200 clusters, and Amazon's vertical integration means the margin on Trainium-run workloads accrues to AWS rather than being transferred to NVIDIA as GPU rental. If Trainium captures 30% of AWS AI compute workloads by FY2028 — plausible given the $225bn obligations imply volumes well above that threshold — and if Trainium unit economics carry an incremental 3-5pp EBIT margin premium versus GPU-rental workloads, the AWS segment margin expands from the base's 36% toward 39-40%. At $255.7bn bull-case revenue, a 39% EBIT margin versus 36% adds ~$7.7bn NOPAT to the bull scenario, worth an additional $7.65/share beyond the pure revenue-growth bull case above.
key_debates.bear_view: Custom silicon is a commodity race that requires perpetual reinvestment. NVIDIA accelerates its roadmap (Blackwell Ultra, Rubin), Google has TPUs, Microsoft is deploying Maia. Amazon has not publicly disclosed Trainium's EBIT margin versus GPU instances; the $20bn run rate may be priced at parity or a discount to GPU instances to drive adoption, meaning the margin premium is assumed rather than observed. The $225bn obligations are purchase obligations (what Amazon committed to buy), not what customers committed to pay.
key_debates.our_verdict: The $225bn obligation clarification is important: it reflects Amazon's own silicon procurement, not customer revenue commitments. However, the strategic logic is correct — internalizing silicon cost versus paying NVIDIA rent is structurally margin-accretive if the workloads exist (and the backlog confirms they do). The margin expansion is likely but not yet observable in the P&L with precision. The bull case earns a 1pp margin expansion assumption (36% to 37%) in the scenario spec, not the full 39% theoretical ceiling. The additional upside above that is optionality.
key_debates.what_would_change_our_mind: AWS Q3 or Q4 2026 reported EBIT margin declining below 36% despite rising revenue, which would signal that Trainium is priced as a loss-leader. Alternatively, a management disclosure that Trainium volumes are below the $20bn run rate claimed in Q1.
key_debates.worth_per_share: $3.72/share from the 1pp margin expansion embedded in the scenario spec (not the full theoretical $7.65). Base uses 36% EBIT margin; scenario spec uses 37% at $255.7bn bull revenue: incremental NOPAT = $255.7bn × 1% × 79% = $2.0bn; at 16.53x exit multiple discounted 2 years: $2.0 × 16.53 / 1.221 = $27.1bn additional EV, or $27.1 / 10.757 = $2.52/share. A full 39% margin adds $7.65/share on top of the revenue upside but is not included in the scenario value.
key_debates.evidence_quality: CONTRACTED — $225bn Trainium purchase obligations and $20bn+ annualized revenue are company-disclosed (Q1 2026, per TIKR/Amazon IR). The margin-expansion read is EXTRAPOLATED: logical and directionally confirmed by rising AWS margins (37.7% Q1 2026, up from 35.4% FY2025), but not separately broken out by silicon type.

key_debates.debate: Does the Prime Video advertising expansion and continued retail media growth lift Amazon Advertising to $110bn+ revenue by FY2027, generating a segment value above the base's $76.61/share — or is the $83.8bn FY2026 run rate already approaching saturation where incremental ad inventory competes with user experience and Google/Meta hold the incremental digital ad dollar?
key_debates.bull_view: Amazon Advertising at $70bn+ TTM (Q1 2026: $17.2bn, +24% YoY) is not yet a saturated market. Amazon holds ~9% of global digital advertising (eMarketer 2026 estimate) against a $900bn+ global digital ad market growing at 12% annually. The structural driver is the migration of product-intent searches from Google to Amazon: a user searching 'buy wireless headphones' on Amazon is further down the purchase funnel than on Google, and advertisers pay for that proximity. Prime Video's ad-supported tier, launched at scale in early 2024, is now contributing 'meaningfully' per Q4 2025 management commentary — this is net-new premium ad inventory that did not exist in the FY2025 base. The bull case for FY2027 advertising revenue of $110.5bn requires a 26-27% CAGR from the Q1 2026 run rate, which is below the 24% growth rate Amazon just posted at $70bn scale. There is no mathematical reason the growth decelerates sharply when the Prime Video inventory layer is just beginning to contribute.
key_debates.bear_view: Google's AI Overviews are pulling product-search queries back into the Google ecosystem. Meta's Advantage+ AI-powered campaigns are demonstrating higher ROI for brand advertisers than Amazon's Sponsored Products. The Prime Video ad inventory carries a natural ceiling — Amazon has 200mn Prime members globally but only a fraction watch Prime Video with the ad tier rather than paying to upgrade. Regulatory pressure on retail media attribution practices (EU Digital Markets Act) could constrain growth in a meaningful geography.
key_debates.our_verdict: The advertising segment is the higher-confidence component of the bull case. The evidence is less reliant on AI ROI materializing — it rests on a structural migration already underway (product-intent search shifting to Amazon) plus a new inventory layer (Prime Video ads) just beginning. The bear's Google AI argument is real but has been real for 18 months without slowing Amazon ad growth. The $110.5bn FY2027 target requires maintaining — not accelerating — the current growth rate.
key_debates.what_would_change_our_mind: Q3 2026 Amazon Advertising growth decelerating below 20% YoY, which would suggest the Prime Video inventory is priced at a discount to existing inventory (dilutive to RPMs) rather than additive. Or a management disclosure of negative Prime Video ad completion rates signaling user backlash.
key_debates.worth_per_share: $16.63/share above base. Advertising bull segment EV: $1,003.0bn ($93.24/share) versus base $824.1bn ($76.61/share). Driven by FY2027 NOPAT rising from $44.3bn (base: $102bn × 55% × 79%) to $48.0bn (bull: $110.5bn × 55% × 79%), with exit multiple expanding from 20.37x to 22.88x via g_lt rising from 5.0% to 5.5% and ROIC from 60% to 65% (Prime Video inventory is near-zero marginal cost, confirming higher reinvestment-free earnings). At 1 year to normalization, discount factor is 1.095.
key_debates.evidence_quality: EXTRAPOLATED — current growth of 24% is observed and documented. The Prime Video contribution is management-confirmed but not separately quantified. The FY2027 revenue target of $110.5bn requires only sustaining, not accelerating, current momentum.

key_debates.debate: Is the stock fairly valued at $237.50 (a 16% premium to the $204 SOTP base) because the market already prices in the bull case, or does the current price represent the market's own base and the bull scenario ($244/share) is still materially above it?
key_debates.bull_view: The current price of $237.50 sits between the base ($204) and bull ($244) scenarios — implying the market is pricing something in between but not the full bull. More precisely, the market is paying $237.50 for a business where: the SOTP base is $204 (no upside from current backlog strength), the bull case adds $40 (backlog conversion at the higher pace plus margin expansion). If AWS grows at 25% through FY2027 and advertising holds 24% growth — both within current run-rate trend — the intrinsic value is closer to $244 than $204. The stock is not cheap in an absolute sense, but it is not pricing the contracted evidence fully either. The Q2 2026 revenue guidance of $194-199bn (both above prior consensus) suggests the near-term trajectory already confirms the bull path rather than base.
key_debates.bear_view: The current price of $237.50 already prices in significant execution. The NTM EV/EBIT of 23.6x versus the justified multiple of 20.4x (from Daedalus's cross-check) implies a 16% premium that requires the bull case to materialize, not just the base. If the base is the honest most-likely, then the stock is slightly expensive at current price. Any miss versus elevated expectations — which the stock's 16% premium embeds — causes a valuation re-rate.
key_debates.our_verdict: The contract backlog of $364bn is not a typical management forecast — it is a legal commitment that Amazon files with the SEC. That the stock at $237.50 does not fully price $244 (the scenario where filed commitments convert at historical rates) suggests the market is still discounting some probability of bear-case outcomes. The bull case argues this discount is too large given the quality of the evidence.
key_debates.what_would_change_our_mind: The bull thesis collapses if Q2 2026 AWS revenue misses the $38.5bn implicit in a 25%+ run rate — any print below $37bn YoY growth would signal deceleration ahead of backlog-supported conversion.
key_debates.worth_per_share: The bull-to-current spread is $6.35/share ($244 - $237.50). The base-to-bull spread is $39.83/share, of which AWS contributes $15.96, Advertising $16.63, NA Retail $7.24. The market is already awarding ~$33.50 of the $39.83 bull case above the base.
key_debates.evidence_quality: EXTRAPOLATED — the current price premium is an observable market fact; the argument that the premium is not yet fully pricing the contracted evidence is a valuation judgment based on the SOTP math.

scenario_spec.key_driver: AWS backlog of $364bn converts at 80-85% historical pace through FY2028, sustaining 26%+ growth versus the base's 17% exit rate in FY2028; Trainium margin expansion (1pp EBIT incremental) is embedded at 37%; Advertising holds 24-26% CAGR on Prime Video inventory ramp. The bull does not require AI ROI proof at enterprise scale — it requires only that signed, filed contracts convert at the rate Amazon has historically delivered.

scenario_lever_attribution.aws_growth_lever: $15.96/share — FY2028 NOPAT from $65.4bn (base) to $72.7bn (bull) at same 16.53x exit multiple, discounted 2 years
scenario_lever_attribution.advertising_lever: $16.63/share — FY2027 NOPAT from $44.3bn (base) to $48.0bn (bull) at 22.88x exit multiple, discounted 1 year
scenario_lever_attribution.trainium_margin_note: 1pp EBIT margin expansion (36% to 37%) is embedded in the aws_ebit_margin_pct above; the full theoretical upside to 39% adds a further $7.65/share but is excluded from the scenario value as HOPED-FOR

conviction_basis: The bull case is grounded in a contracted, filed backlog number that is extraordinary in its magnitude and sequential acceleration. The conversion math is well-supported. The reason for MEDIUM rather than HIGH is that (a) the backlog-to-revenue conversion timing is extrapolated from historical cloud patterns, not contractually guaranteed on a quarterly basis; (b) the Trainium margin expansion is directionally confirmed by Q1 2026 margin records but not yet observable as a silicon-specific P&L line; and (c) at $237.50, the stock already prices ~84% of the distance from the $204 base to the $244 bull, leaving limited margin for surprise. The bull thesis is well-evidenced but most of it is already in the price.
